modules.title: Flooring Fundamentals
learningObjectives: Understand construction and layer composition of LVP, engineered hardwood, tile, and laminate
Explain how material construction affects installation requirements and failure modes
Describe thermal expansion and moisture dynamics in flooring systems
Identify substrate-flooring interaction failures and their prevention
Apply Climate Zone 6 considerations to product selection and installation timing

criticalStandards.description: SPC cores are dimensionally stable but transmit sound; WPC cores are quieter but dent under point loads
Engineered hardwood veneer below 2mm cannot be refinished — sanding will burn through
Porcelain adhesion is purely mechanical — back-buttering is critical
Laminate HDF core swells permanently when exposed to moisture — never returns to original dimension
Expansion gaps are calculated clearances for worst-case seasonal movement, not safety margins
Climate Zone 6 indoor RH swings 30-40% seasonally — acclimate to average annual condition (35-45% RH)

content.introduction: Before installing any flooring, you need to understand what you're working with and why it behaves the way it does. This section covers the construction of each flooring type, the building science that governs installation success, and the specific challenges of working in Climate Zone 6.

sections.title: Product Construction & Performance
sections.body: Every flooring type has a distinct construction that determines its performance characteristics, installation requirements, and failure modes. Understanding these fundamentals helps you anticipate problems before they occur.

subsections.title: Luxury Vinyl Plank (LVP) Construction
subsections.body: LVP consists of multiple layers fused together under heat and pressure. The wear layer thickness (12-28 mil residential) determines longevity — not the overall plank thickness.

tables.caption: LVP Layer Breakdown
rows: Scratch, stain, UV protection (measured in mils)

callouts.title: SPC vs WPC
callouts.content: SPC cores are denser and more dimensionally stable but transmit more sound. WPC cores are softer underfoot and quieter but can dent under heavy point loads.

subsections.title: Engineered Hardwood Construction
subsections.body: Engineered hardwood uses a cross-laminated construction similar to plywood. The cross-laminated core constrains wood movement to approximately 50% of solid hardwood. However, it still moves — especially across the width. A 5" engineered plank can still gain or lose 1/16" seasonally.

callouts.title: Refinishability
callouts.content: The veneer thickness determines if the floor can be refinished: below 2mm, sanding will burn through.

subsections.title: Ceramic & Porcelain Tile Construction
subsections.body: Tiles are made from clay fired at high temperatures. The firing temperature and clay composition determine the tile type.

callouts.title: Porcelain Adhesion
callouts.content: Porcelain's near-zero absorption means it won't wick moisture, but it also means thinset can't penetrate the tile body — adhesion is purely mechanical. This is why back-buttering is critical for porcelain.
Ceramic tiles absorb some thinset moisture, creating a stronger chemical bond but making them unsuitable for freeze-thaw environments.

subsections.title: Laminate Flooring Construction
subsections.body: Laminate flooring consists of four main layers. The HDF core is the critical vulnerability — it's made from wood fibers bonded with resin, essentially compressed sawdust.

callouts.title: Moisture Vulnerability
callouts.content: Water penetration at edges or through the surface causes irreversible swelling. Unlike solid wood that can dry out, swollen HDF never returns to its original dimension. This is why edge sealing and avoiding wet areas is non-negotiable for laminate.

sections.title: Building Science for Installers
sections.body: Flooring failures are physics failures. Understanding how heat, moisture, and materials interact prevents callbacks and protects your reputation.

subsections.title: Thermal Expansion & Contraction
subsections.body: All materials expand when heated and contract when cooled. The rate of expansion varies by material. The problem: Flooring expands at a different rate than the substrate and surrounding structure. When expansion is constrained (floor pinned against a wall, under a cabinet, or screwed through), the energy has nowhere to go — it converts to vertical displacement. This is buckling.

callouts.title: The Expansion Gap Principle
callouts.content: Expansion gaps aren't optional safety margins — they're calculated clearances that accommodate the worst-case seasonal movement for your flooring type. A 1/4" gap on LVP isn't conservative; it's the minimum physics requires.

subsections.title: Moisture Dynamics
subsections.body: Moisture moves from high concentration to low concentration (wet to dry) and from warm to cold. In flooring, this creates several effects:

callouts.content: Hygroscopic expansion: Wood fibers absorb moisture from humid air and swell. This is different from thermal expansion — it's chemical absorption.
Vapor drive: Moisture in concrete migrates upward toward the drier interior. Without a vapor barrier, this moisture enters wood-based flooring from below.
Cupping vs crowning: When the bottom of a board is wetter than the top, edges curl up (cupping). When the top is wetter, the center rises (crowning). Both indicate moisture imbalance.
Equilibrium moisture content (EMC): Wood will gain or lose moisture until it reaches equilibrium with surrounding air. Installation should occur when the wood is at its expected in-service EMC.

subsections.title: Substrate-Flooring Interaction
subsections.body: The substrate and flooring are a system, not independent layers. Understanding common interaction failures helps prevent them.

tables.caption: Common Interaction Failures
rows: Substrate imperfections visible through thin flooring
Flatten substrate, use thicker flooring or underlayment
Voids in thinset from improper coverage or substrate suction
Prime substrate, back-butter, work in small sections
Bond breaks due to moisture, contamination, or incompatibility
Test moisture, clean substrate, verify adhesive compatibility
Substrate and flooring expand/contract at different rates
Movement joints, flexible adhesives, floating installation

sections.title: Climate Zone 6 Considerations
sections.body: New Brunswick sits in Climate Zone 6, which means cold winters, significant heating seasons, and dramatic seasonal humidity swings. This creates specific challenges for flooring installation.

subsections.title: Seasonal Humidity Swings
subsections.body: In Climate Zone 6, indoor relative humidity can swing from 25-30% in winter (heated air is dry) to 60-70% in summer. This 30-40% RH swing causes significant dimensional changes in wood-based products.

callouts.title: Installation Timing
callouts.content: If you install hardwood in humid August at 50% RH, and the home drops to 25% RH in January, you'll see gapping. If you install in dry February and summer humidity hits, you risk buckling. Best practice is to acclimate to the home's average annual condition (typically 35-45% RH with HVAC running).

subsections.title: Concrete Moisture in Cold Climates
subsections.body: Concrete slabs in Climate Zone 6 face unique moisture challenges:

callouts.content: Below-grade slabs: Constant contact with cold, damp soil. Vapor drive is year-round, not just seasonal.
New construction: Concrete needs 60-90 days to cure and release moisture. Winter pours with enclosed heating can trap moisture.
Freeze-thaw at slab edges: Moisture at slab perimeter can freeze, causing heaving and cracking that affects flooring above.

callouts.title: Climate Zone 6 Rule
callouts.content: Always perform moisture testing on concrete slabs in Climate Zone 6, regardless of slab age. Vapor barriers are mandatory for any wood-based flooring installed below grade. Assume vapor drive is present unless testing proves otherwise.

subsections.title: Product Selection for Climate Zone 6
subsections.body: Some products are better suited for Climate Zone 6 conditions than others, particularly for below-grade and on-grade applications.

tables.caption: Product Selection by Application
rows: LVP, tile, engineered with moisture barrier

modules.title: Substrate Assessment & Preparation
learningObjectives: Apply the four-point substrate check (Clean, Dry, Flat, Sound) to any subfloor
Perform and interpret moisture testing using pin meter and RH probe methods
Measure flatness using an 80" long level and identify correction requirements
Select and apply appropriate correction methods for common substrate defects

criticalStandards.description: Never proceed with installation if moisture readings exceed manufacturer specifications
Wood subfloor: ≤12% MC, Concrete: ≤75% RH or ≤3 lbs/1000 sq ft MVER
Flatness tolerance: 3/16" in 10' for standard installations, 1/8" in 10' for large format tile

content.introduction: The substrate is the foundation of every flooring installation. A perfectly installed floor will fail if the substrate is not properly prepared. This module covers the systematic assessment and correction of substrates before any flooring goes down.

procedures.title: Four-Point Substrate Check
procedures.body: Every substrate must pass four checks before installation begins. Failing any one requires correction before proceeding.
steps: CLEAN: Verify substrate is free of debris, dust, adhesive residue, paint, oil, and any contaminants that could interfere with adhesion or underlayment performance
DRY: Test moisture content using appropriate method for substrate type — pin meter for wood, RH probe or calcium chloride for concrete
FLAT: Check flatness using 80" long level {{LABS:80-inch-long-level}}, marking high and low spots — tolerance depends on flooring type
SOUND: Walk the entire surface listening and feeling for squeaks, deflection, loose panels, or hollow areas that indicate structural issues

procedures.title: Moisture Testing — Wood Subfloor
procedures.body: Wood subfloor moisture is measured with a pin-type moisture meter. The pins penetrate the wood surface to measure electrical resistance, which correlates to moisture content.
steps: Calibrate pin meter {{LABS:pin-moisture-meter}} according to manufacturer instructions
Set meter to correct wood species (or use correction chart for engineered panels)
Take readings at minimum 3 locations per 500 sq ft — more in suspect areas
Insert pins to 1/4" depth, perpendicular to grain
Record readings — acceptable range is 6-12% MC for most hardwood installations
Check differential: flooring MC should be within 2-4% of subfloor MC

callouts.content: Pin meters read surface moisture only. If you suspect deeper moisture issues (recent water damage, below-grade), use extended probes or wait and retest.

procedures.title: Moisture Testing — Concrete Slab
procedures.body: Concrete moisture testing is more complex because moisture is always present in concrete — the question is how much is moving. Two primary methods are used.
steps: RH Probe Method (ASTM F2170): Drill holes to 40% of slab depth, install sleeves, wait 72 hours for equilibration, insert probe and read — limit is typically 75-80% RH
Calcium Chloride Method (ASTM F1869): Seal test kit to clean, bare concrete, wait 60-72 hours, weigh — limit is typically 3 lbs/1000 sq ft/24 hours
Test at minimum 3 locations per 1000 sq ft — more near exterior walls, plumbing, and known wet areas
For below-grade slabs in Climate Zone 6, always test regardless of slab age

callouts.title: Which Test to Use
callouts.content: RH probes are more accurate and measure the moisture that will actually reach the flooring. Calcium chloride measures surface emissions but can be affected by ambient conditions. Most manufacturers now specify RH probe results.
If moisture exceeds limits, do not proceed. Options: wait and retest, apply moisture mitigation system {{LABS:moisture-mitigation}}, or change flooring specification to moisture-tolerant product.

procedures.title: Flatness Check
procedures.body: Flatness is measured with a long straightedge or level, checking the gap between the tool and the floor surface.
steps: Use 80" long level {{LABS:80-inch-long-level}} — longer than typical 10' straightedge but more practical for transport
Place level on floor and check gap at center using feeler gauge or spacer
Work systematically across room in grid pattern, marking high spots (circle) and low spots (X)
Standard tolerance: 3/16" in 10' (or proportionally 1/8" in 80" level length)
Large format tile (15"+): 1/8" in 10' (proportionally 3/32" in 80")
Document locations and magnitude of out-of-tolerance areas

procedures.title: Substrate Correction — Leveling
procedures.body: Low spots and overall unevenness are corrected with leveling compounds. Product selection depends on the depth of correction needed and the substrate type.
steps: For corrections under 1/4": Use patch compound applied with trowel {{LABS:floor-patch}}
For corrections 1/4" to 1": Use self-leveling compound {{LABS:self-leveling-compound}}
For corrections over 1": Build up in lifts or use lightweight leveler
Prime substrate before leveling per manufacturer instructions
Mix leveler to correct consistency — too thin runs off, too thick won't self-level
Work in sections you can complete before material sets
Allow full cure time before flooring installation (typically 24-48 hours)

callouts.content: Pour self-leveler on a primed floor. The primer controls absorption — without it, the substrate sucks water from the leveler too fast, causing cracking and poor adhesion.

modules.title: Material Handling & Acclimation
learningObjectives: Calculate acclimation requirements based on product type and site conditions
Properly store and protect flooring materials on job site
Verify material condition and identify defects before installation
Document acclimation readings and maintain chain of custody

criticalStandards.description: Acclimate hardwood until MC is within 2% of subfloor MC and within expected in-service range
Never store flooring directly on concrete — use pallets, sleepers, or vapor barrier
HVAC must be operating at normal conditions during acclimation — not just heating, but humidity control

content.introduction: Flooring materials must reach equilibrium with their installed environment before installation. Skipping or shortcutting acclimation is the leading cause of flooring failures — and failures that occur months after installation, after you've been paid and moved on.

procedures.title: Acclimation Requirements by Product
procedures.body: Different products have different acclimation needs. Some require extended acclimation, others need almost none.

tables.caption: Acclimation Requirements
rows: Longer in Climate Zone 6 winter/summer extremes
Cross-ply construction reduces movement but still needed
Dimensional stability high, but cold vinyl is brittle
Inert material, but store flat to prevent warping

procedures.title: Job Site Storage
procedures.body: Proper storage protects materials during acclimation and prevents damage that won't be visible until after installation.
steps: Never store flooring directly on concrete — moisture will migrate into packaging. Use pallets, sleepers, or 6-mil poly
Keep materials away from exterior walls where temperature swings are greatest
Do not store in garages, unheated spaces, or areas without climate control
Leave cartons sealed until ready to install — open cartons absorb/release moisture faster than expected
Cross-stack or sticker solid hardwood to allow air circulation on all surfaces
Protect from direct sunlight which can cause uneven drying and color fading

callouts.title: Climate Control Required
callouts.content: The job site HVAC must be operating at normal occupied conditions during acclimation. A house that's been sitting at 55°F with no humidity control is not ready for flooring delivery. Require HVAC operation for 5+ days before material arrives.

procedures.title: Acclimation Monitoring
procedures.body: Acclimation isn't just about time — it's about reaching the target moisture content. Measure and document.
steps: Record initial MC of flooring when delivered (open one carton from each lot)
Record subfloor MC at same time
Check flooring MC every 24-48 hours until readings stabilize
Target: Flooring MC within 2% of subfloor MC, and within 6-9% for most species
Document all readings with date, time, location, and ambient conditions
Do not begin installation until target is reached — time alone is not sufficient

procedures.title: Pre-Installation Inspection
procedures.body: Before installing any material, inspect for defects. Issues found after installation are your problem; issues documented beforehand are the supplier's problem.
steps: Open all cartons 24 hours before installation
Inspect each piece under good lighting for: color consistency, finish defects, dimensional issues, damage
Set aside any questionable pieces — do not install defects
Photograph defects and notify supplier before proceeding
Mix pieces from multiple cartons during installation to distribute color variation
Keep 2-3 cartons in reserve for warranty replacement

callouts.title: 5% Rule
callouts.content: Most manufacturers allow up to 5% defects in a shipment. If you find more than 5%, stop and contact the supplier before installation. Installing more than 5% of a defective lot may void your warranty claim.

modules.title: Layout Planning & Design
learningObjectives: Calculate material quantities including waste factors for different patterns
Plan layout to minimize awkward cuts and maximize visual appeal
Establish chalk lines and reference points for straight, square installation
Handle transitions, obstacles, and irregular room shapes

criticalStandards.description: First row must be perfectly straight — all subsequent rows follow this reference
Minimum plank width at walls: 1/3 of full plank width (no slivers)
Stagger end joints minimum 6" for plank, 50% offset for tile (brick pattern)

content.introduction: Layout determines how professional the finished floor looks. Poor layout results in awkward cuts, slivers at walls, misaligned patterns, and visual chaos. Good layout is invisible — the floor looks like it was meant to be there.

procedures.title: Material Calculation
procedures.body: Always order more material than the room's square footage. Waste factors depend on room complexity and pattern.

callouts.content: Calculate room square footage from longest points, including closets and alcoves. Then add waste factor. Always round up to full carton quantities — partial cartons aren't available.

procedures.title: Layout Planning
procedures.body: Before snapping a single chalk line, walk the room and plan how the flooring will flow.
steps: Identify the primary sight line — usually the main entrance to the room or the longest wall
Measure room width perpendicular to intended plank direction
Calculate how many full planks fit, and what the last row width will be
If last row would be less than 1/3 plank width, adjust starting row — rip first row narrower to balance
Plan for doorways: flooring should run through door openings, not terminate awkwardly
Consider furniture placement: seams under beds/couches are acceptable, seams in main traffic paths are not

callouts.title: The Racking Rule
callouts.content: When opening cartons, sort planks by length if they vary. Start and end rows with longer pieces. Use shorter pieces in closets and secondary areas. This prevents a section of floor with disproportionately many end joints.

procedures.title: Establishing Reference Lines
procedures.body: Chalk lines establish the reference that all flooring follows. If the lines are wrong, the floor is wrong.
steps: Snap a chalk line parallel to the longest or most visible wall — this is your primary reference
Check if walls are parallel by measuring at multiple points. Walls are rarely perfectly parallel
If walls are out of parallel, split the difference so cuts at both ends are similar
Snap a perpendicular line using 3-4-5 triangle method (or multiples: 6-8-10, 9-12-15)
Verify 90° angle at intersection before proceeding
For tile: snap a grid of layout lines at intervals matching your tile size plus grout joint

callouts.title: Chalk Lines vs Laser Lines
callouts.content: Chalk lines create a permanent reference mark on the substrate. Laser lines are for active alignment during work. You need both: chalk lines for the starting reference, laser level for ongoing alignment checks.

procedures.title: Handling Obstacles
procedures.body: Every room has obstacles: door frames, pipes, columns, floor vents. Plan for them.
steps: Door frames: Undercut jambs and casings to slide flooring beneath — do not notch flooring around trim
Pipes: Drill hole 1/2" larger than pipe diameter, cut plank to hole, slide in place, cover with escutcheon
Floor vents: Center vents in plank where possible, or adjust layout so vent falls at joint between planks
Columns/posts: Box around with even reveal on all sides, or run flooring to edge and cap with trim
Transitions: Plan transition strip locations at doorways, material changes, and at 30-40' intervals for expansion

modules.title: LVP/LVT Installation
learningObjectives: Execute click-lock floating installation with proper expansion gaps
Make accurate cuts using appropriate tools for straight, angled, and curved cuts
Install transitions, reducers, and edge trim correctly
Troubleshoot common click-lock engagement issues

criticalStandards.description: Expansion gap: 1/4" minimum at all walls, fixed cabinets, and vertical obstructions
Click joints must fully engage with audible click — gaps indicate incomplete lock
Maximum run without transition: 30-40 feet depending on manufacturer specifications

content.introduction: Luxury Vinyl Plank (LVP) and Luxury Vinyl Tile (LVT) are the most common flooring we install. The click-lock floating installation is forgiving of minor substrate imperfections and can be installed over most existing floors. But the basics still matter: flat substrate, proper expansion gaps, and fully engaged joints.

procedures.title: Pre-Installation Verification
procedures.body: Before starting installation, verify all prerequisites are met.
steps: Substrate passes four-point check (Clean, Dry, Flat, Sound)
Material has acclimated 24-48 hours at room temperature
Cartons opened and inspected for defects
Layout planned, chalk lines snapped
All door jambs undercut
Expansion materials on hand: spacers, transition strips, shoe molding

procedures.title: First Row Installation
procedures.body: The first row establishes the reference for the entire floor. Take your time.
steps: Place 1/4" spacers along starting wall
If first row needs to be ripped (to balance last row), rip tongue side off first row pieces
Lay first plank with tongue facing room, groove against spacers
Connect end joints by angling plank and dropping into previous plank's end lock
Use pull bar for last piece in row where tapping block won't fit
Check first row alignment with chalk line — adjust before proceeding
First row must be dead straight. If it curves, every subsequent row will follow the curve

callouts.content: On long runs, tack the first row in place with a few pieces of blue tape to the substrate. This prevents the row from drifting while you work down the room.

procedures.title: Field Installation
procedures.body: Once the first row is set, field installation proceeds quickly.
steps: Start each row with the cutoff from the previous row's end (if 6"+ long)
Stagger end joints minimum 6" from adjacent rows — random stagger looks more natural than H-pattern
Angle long edge into previous row at 20-30°, then press down to engage lock
Listen for click — if no click, disengage and check for debris or misalignment
Use tapping block on short end to close end joints — never hammer directly on plank edge
Check every 3-4 rows: straight, tight joints, consistent expansion gap at walls

callouts.title: No Adhesive
callouts.content: Floating LVP is designed to float. Never glue planks to substrate or to each other unless specifically required by manufacturer for glue-down installations. Adhesive prevents natural expansion/contraction.

procedures.title: Cutting Techniques
procedures.body: Accurate cuts are essential for professional results. LVP cuts easily with the right tools.

tables.caption: Cutting Methods
rows: Score and snap for clean break
Mark carefully, cut just outside line
Drill starter hole, cut from inside

callouts.content: A dedicated LVP cutter {{LABS:lvp-cutter}} makes hundreds of clean crosscuts per day with no dust, no noise, no blade changes. Worth the investment for any volume work.

procedures.title: Transitions & Trim
procedures.body: Transitions handle changes in elevation, material, or direction. They also provide expansion relief on long runs.
steps: T-molding: Use where two floating floors meet at same height (doorways between rooms)
Reducer: Use where LVP meets lower surface (tile, concrete, low-pile carpet)
End cap: Use where floor ends at vertical surface (fireplace, sliding door track)
Stair nose: Use at stair edges — must be glued, not floating
Install transition track first, then snap or slide transition piece into track
Leave 1/4" expansion gap between flooring and transition track

procedures.title: Final Row & Completion
procedures.body: The last row often requires ripping to fit.
steps: Measure from last full row to wall at multiple points (walls aren't straight)
Transfer measurements to plank, subtracting 1/4" for expansion gap
Rip planks to width using table saw or score-and-snap method
Install using pull bar since tapping block won't fit against wall
Install shoe molding or quarter round to cover expansion gap — nail to wall, not to floor
Remove all spacers
Clean floor with manufacturer-recommended cleaner

callouts.title: Shoe Molding Rule
callouts.content: Shoe molding must be nailed to the wall/base, never to the floor. If attached to the floor, it pins the edge and prevents expansion. You will get callbacks.

modules.title: Engineered Hardwood Installation
learningObjectives: Select appropriate installation method (float, glue-down, nail-down) based on substrate and conditions
Execute floating installation with proper underlayment and expansion control
Perform glue-down installation with correct adhesive coverage and working time
Install nail-down over wood substrate with proper fastener schedule

criticalStandards.description: Nail-down requires minimum 3/4" plywood or OSB subfloor — never nail into concrete
Glue coverage minimum 95% — check by lifting pieces periodically during installation
Floating engineered: expansion gap 3/8" minimum, transition every 30' in any direction

content.introduction: Engineered hardwood offers the beauty of real wood with better dimensional stability than solid hardwood. It can be installed using three methods: floating, glue-down, or nail-down. The right method depends on the substrate, the product specifications, and the desired feel underfoot.

procedures.title: Installation Method Selection
procedures.body: Each method has advantages and requirements. Match the method to the conditions.

tables.caption: Installation Methods Comparison
rows: Any flat surface (concrete, plywood, existing floor)

callouts.content: Most engineered products allow multiple installation methods. Check manufacturer specs to confirm which methods are approved for your specific product.

procedures.title: Floating Installation
procedures.body: Floating installation connects planks to each other but not to the substrate. The floor moves as a unit on top of the underlayment.
steps: Install appropriate underlayment — foam for basic, cork for sound reduction, film-faced for moisture barrier
Overlap underlayment seams 4-6", tape seams with vapor-retardant tape
Place 3/8" spacers at all walls (engineered needs more gap than LVP)
Install first row groove-to-wall, connecting end joints as you go
Engage tongue-and-groove by angling plank and lowering — some require tapping
Stagger end joints minimum 6" (longer for wide plank — 8-12")
Install transitions at doorways and at 30' maximum runs

callouts.title: Underlayment Over Concrete
callouts.content: When floating over concrete, use underlayment with integrated vapor barrier or install separate 6-mil poly. Moisture will migrate through concrete and into engineered hardwood without protection.

procedures.title: Glue-Down Installation
procedures.body: Glue-down creates a solid, quiet floor with no flex. It requires more preparation and is less forgiving of mistakes.
steps: Substrate must be perfectly flat — 1/8" in 10' maximum variance
Spread adhesive with recommended trowel notch size (typically 1/4" x 1/4" square notch)
Work in sections you can cover in adhesive working time (typically 30-60 minutes)
Lay planks into wet adhesive, sliding tongue into groove
Press firmly — do not pound. Adhesive needs contact, not impact
Check coverage periodically by lifting a plank — adhesive should contact 95%+ of back
Roll floor with 100 lb roller after installation, then again the next day

callouts.title: Working Time
callouts.content: Adhesive has open time (how long before it skins) and working time (how long you can install on it). Do not spread more than you can cover. If adhesive skins over, scrape it up and reapply.
For glue-down, use painter's tape along joints as you install to prevent adhesive squeeze-up. Peel tape before adhesive cures.

procedures.title: Nail-Down Installation
procedures.body: Nail-down is the traditional method for hardwood. It requires a wood substrate and a pneumatic flooring nailer.
steps: Verify substrate is minimum 3/4" plywood or OSB, properly fastened to joists
Snap chalk line for first row, maintaining 3/8" expansion gap at starting wall
Face-nail first row through tongue at 6" intervals (predrill to prevent splitting)
After 2-3 rows, switch to pneumatic flooring nailer {{LABS:flooring-nailer}}
Position nailer on tongue at 45° angle, 1-2" from end joints, 6-8" along plank
Seat cleats firmly but don't overdrive — overdriven cleats split tongues
Continue nailing every 6-8" on center, with nail within 2" of each end
Last 2-3 rows: return to face-nailing, fill holes with color-matched putty

callouts.title: Air Pressure
callouts.content: Flooring nailer air pressure is critical. Too low: cleats don't seat. Too high: cleats blow through tongue. Test on scrap until cleats seat flush with top of tongue.

modules.title: Ceramic & Porcelain Tile Installation
learningObjectives: Select appropriate thinset type for substrate and tile combination
Achieve minimum 95% coverage with proper trowel technique and back-buttering
Set tiles flat and level using appropriate leveling systems
Grout joints correctly and seal as required

criticalStandards.description: Large format tile (15"+ on any side) requires minimum 95% coverage with no voids
Back-butter all porcelain tile — surface texture and low absorption require it
Lippage tolerance: 1/32" for tile edges under 15", 1/16" for 15"+

content.introduction: Tile installation demands precision. Unlike floating floors that forgive minor imperfections, tile locks every mistake in place. But done right, tile is the most durable flooring available — decades of service with minimal maintenance.

procedures.title: Thinset Selection
procedures.body: Not all thinsets are equal. Match the thinset to your substrate, tile type, and conditions.

tables.caption: Thinset Types
rows: Portland cement + sand, mix with water

callouts.title: Modified Over Cement Board
callouts.content: Some cement board manufacturers prohibit modified thinset directly on their product — it can create adhesion problems. Always check substrate manufacturer specs.

procedures.title: Troweling & Coverage
procedures.body: Trowel technique determines coverage. Coverage determines whether tiles crack or survive. This is the most important tile skill.
steps: Mix thinset to creamy peanut butter consistency — not too wet, not too dry
Apply thinset to substrate with flat side of trowel, pressing firmly to key into surface
Comb with notched side at consistent 45° angle in ONE direction (not swirls)
Trowel notch size: 1/4" x 1/4" for tiles up to 12", 1/2" x 1/2" for larger tiles
For large format tile: back-butter the tile with thin skim coat in addition to substrate coverage
Set tile into combed thinset, slide slightly perpendicular to ridges, then press and twist to collapse ridges
Periodically lift a tile to check coverage — 80% minimum for standard, 95% for large format

callouts.title: Why One Direction?
callouts.content: Combing in one direction allows air to escape when you set the tile. Swirl patterns trap air pockets that become voids — and voids crack under load.

procedures.title: Setting & Leveling
procedures.body: Getting tiles flat relative to each other (minimizing lippage) requires a system, not just eyeballing.
steps: Start from layout lines, working outward so you're never kneeling on set tiles
Use tile leveling system {{LABS:tile-leveling-system}} for any tile over 12" — clips go under tile edges, wedges between clips
Set tile with slight twisting motion to collapse thinset ridges
Insert spacers between tiles for consistent grout joints
Check level with straightedge spanning multiple tiles — adjust by pressing high corners
Do not walk on tiles for 24 hours (or per thinset specifications)

callouts.content: Tile leveling clips are an investment, but they pay for themselves in eliminated callbacks. Large format tile without a leveling system is gambling.

procedures.title: Grouting
procedures.body: Grout fills joints, prevents water intrusion, and provides the final appearance. Timing is critical.
steps: Wait minimum 24 hours after setting (48+ for large format) before grouting
Mix grout per manufacturer specs — consistency of thick oatmeal
Work grout into joints with rubber float held at 45°, moving diagonally to joints
Pack joints completely with no voids or air pockets
Wait 10-20 minutes until grout firms, then wipe diagonally with damp sponge
Rinse sponge frequently, don't over-wet — too much water washes out portland
Second sponge pass to remove haze, then polish with dry cloth
Seal grout after full cure (72 hours+) if using cement-based grout

callouts.title: Don't Overwork
callouts.content: Wiping too soon washes grout out of joints. Wiping too late makes haze impossible to remove. Watch the surface — when it starts to firm, start wiping.

modules.title: Laminate Installation
learningObjectives: Execute click-lock floating installation with proper underlayment and vapor protection
Make clean cuts without chipping decorative surface
Protect edges from moisture in kitchens and bathrooms when laminate is approved
Install transitions and trim to complete the installation

criticalStandards.description: Laminate requires 6-mil poly vapor barrier over ALL concrete substrates
Edge seal all cut edges exposed to potential moisture (sink bases, dishwashers)
Expansion gap: 5/16" minimum at all walls — more than LVP due to HDF movement

content.introduction: Laminate flooring offers the look of wood at a lower price point, with good durability for its cost. The critical difference from LVP is the HDF core — it must be protected from moisture at all times. Even brief water exposure can cause permanent swelling.

procedures.title: Substrate Preparation
procedures.body: Laminate has the same basic requirements as LVP but is less forgiving of moisture issues.
steps: Verify substrate passes four-point check (Clean, Dry, Flat, Sound)
Over concrete: ALWAYS install 6-mil poly vapor barrier, overlap seams 8", tape seams
Install underlayment — foam minimum, cork for sound, film-faced for additional moisture protection
Underlayment seams must not align with laminate seams — offset by half width
Verify all moisture issues are resolved before laminate arrives — no exceptions

callouts.title: Moisture Zero Tolerance
callouts.content: Unlike LVP which can tolerate some moisture exposure, laminate HDF core swells irreversibly when wet. There is no repair. Prevention is the only strategy.

procedures.title: Installation
procedures.body: Laminate installation follows similar process to LVP with slightly larger expansion gaps.
steps: Open cartons 48 hours before installation for acclimation
Plan layout to avoid slivers at walls — minimum 2" final row width
Place 5/16" spacers at all walls (larger than LVP due to HDF expansion)
Install first row with tongue facing room, groove against spacers
Angle planks into previous row, press down to engage click
Use tapping block on tongue side only — never hit groove edge directly
Stagger end joints minimum 8" from adjacent rows

procedures.title: Cutting
procedures.body: Laminate's decorative layer can chip if cut incorrectly. Tool and technique matter.
steps: For crosscuts: use miter saw with fine-tooth blade (80+), decorative side UP
For rip cuts: use table saw with fine-tooth blade, decorative side UP
Alternative: score decorative surface deeply with utility knife, then cut from back — prevents chipping
For jigsaw cuts: use reverse-tooth blade OR cut from back side
Clean all edges after cutting — debris in joints prevents click engagement

callouts.content: Blue painter's tape applied over cut line reduces chipping on decorative surface. Remove tape after cutting.

procedures.title: Moisture Protection in Kitchens
procedures.body: Laminate is generally not recommended for full kitchens, but if specified, edge protection is mandatory.
steps: Seal all cut edges with laminate edge sealer {{LABS:laminate-edge-sealer}} before installation
At sink base and dishwasher: create water dam with silicone bead under cabinet toe kick
Consider removing laminate 1" into cabinet area and installing vinyl or tile under appliances
Install threshold or transition at kitchen entrance to isolate potential water damage
Document moisture protection measures for warranty purposes

callouts.content: Many laminate warranties exclude water damage regardless of source. Even with protection, customers should understand the risk of laminate in wet areas.

modules.title: Transitions & Trim Work
learningObjectives: Select appropriate transition type for each condition (elevation change, material change, expansion)
Install transitions with proper expansion gaps and secure attachment
Install base molding and shoe molding without pinning floating floors
Create professional details at stairs, fireplace hearths, and sliding doors

criticalStandards.description: Transition tracks must be fastened to substrate, not to flooring — allow 1/4" gap
Shoe molding fastens to base molding only, never to floor — prevents pinning
Stair nosings must be glued and mechanically fastened — no floating stair details

content.introduction: Transitions and trim are where the flooring meets the rest of the house. Poor trim work undermines even the best floor installation. Good trim work makes the floor look intentional, professional, and finished.

procedures.title: Transition Types
procedures.body: Each transition type serves a specific purpose. Using the wrong transition creates problems.

tables.caption: Transition Selection Guide
rows: Two floors at same height (doorway between rooms)
Track fastened to substrate, T snaps in
Floor to lower surface (vinyl to tile, floor to carpet)
Floor ends at vertical surface (fireplace, slider track)
Pins carpet edge, covers floor edge

procedures.title: Transition Installation
procedures.body: Proper transition installation maintains expansion gaps while providing a clean appearance.
steps: Measure and cut transition track to fit opening precisely
Position track centered on floor change, ensuring 1/4" expansion gap from flooring
Fasten track to substrate with screws (wood) or concrete screws (concrete)
Verify flooring can slide under transition without binding
Cut transition piece to length — miter for angled transitions
Snap or slide transition into track — should fit snugly without forcing
At doorways, install transition directly under closed door

callouts.title: Transition Location
callouts.content: Transitions at doorways should fall directly under the door when closed. When the door opens, the transition is visible; when closed, it's hidden. This is the professional standard.

procedures.title: Base Molding & Shoe Molding
procedures.body: Base molding covers the expansion gap. Shoe molding covers any remaining gap at the floor line and allows for floor thickness variations.
steps: Measure and cut base molding to fit walls — miter inside corners, cope inside corners for tight fit (preferred)
Attach base molding to wall studs with finish nails — do not attach to floor
Base should sit on floor but not press down — 1/16" gap is acceptable
Cut shoe molding to match base runs — miter all corners
Attach shoe to base molding only, using brads — never nail into floor
If shoe doesn't reach floor, shim base up slightly or use taller shoe profile

callouts.title: Nail Direction Matters
callouts.content: Shoe molding nails angle upward into the base, not downward into the floor. Any fastener into a floating floor will cause buckling when the floor tries to expand.

procedures.title: Special Conditions
procedures.body: Some areas require specific approaches beyond standard transitions.
steps: Fireplace hearth: Use reducer or end cap. If hearth is raised, flooring can tuck under if gap allows. Otherwise, end cap provides clean termination
Sliding door tracks: End cap flush with track, ensuring door operation isn't impeded. Leave room for thermal expansion of track
Floor vents: Adjust layout so vents fall on joints if possible. Use vent extenders if floor raises vent above surrounding carpet
Stair nosings: Glue with construction adhesive, then screw through nosing into tread. Fill screw holes with color-matched filler. Never rely on adhesive alone
Floating-to-nail transition: When floating floor meets nailed hardwood, use T-molding with gap on floating side only

modules.title: Quality Inspection & Defect Prevention
learningObjectives: Perform systematic visual inspection using appropriate lighting conditions
Execute functional tests for click engagement, flatness, and stability
Identify common defects and trace them to root causes
Apply prevention strategies to avoid callbacks and warranty issues

criticalStandards.description: Inspect all flooring under natural light from normal standing height — the customer's perspective
Document all pre-existing conditions before installation — photos with timestamps
Customer walkthrough required before final payment — sign-off acknowledges acceptance

content.introduction: Quality isn't inspected into a floor — it's built in. But inspection confirms that quality is present and creates documentation that protects everyone. A systematic approach catches issues before the customer does.

procedures.title: Visual Inspection Protocol
procedures.body: Visual inspection should be done in conditions that match how the customer will see the floor.

checkpoints.acceptable: Uniform color, pattern flows naturally, no obvious defects
checkpoints.unacceptable: Color banding, mismatched planks, visible damage

checkpoints.acceptable: Joints tight with no visible gaps (hairline acceptable for wood)
checkpoints.unacceptable: Gaps visible from standing height, separation between planks

checkpoints.acceptable: No visible high edges or humps under normal lighting
checkpoints.unacceptable: Lippage visible under normal lighting, tripping hazards

checkpoints.acceptable: Centered, flush or smooth height change, no sharp edges
Tight to floor and walls, miters closed, consistent reveal
checkpoints.unacceptable: Gaps under trim, open miters, visible nails

checkpoints.acceptable: Covered by trim, expansion gap maintained
checkpoints.unacceptable: Cut edges visible beyond trim, floor touching walls

procedures.title: Functional Tests
procedures.body: Beyond visual, test that the floor performs as expected.

tests.method: Walk entire floor listening and feeling for loose joints
Roll office chair or appliance dolly across floor
Stand at edge and rock weight side to side
Walk entire floor focusing on areas over subfloor seams
Open and close all doors, check room-to-room flow

procedures.title: Common Defects & Prevention
procedures.body: Understanding defects helps prevent them. Most issues trace to a handful of root causes.

tables.caption: Flooring Defects Reference
rows: Insufficient acclimation, dry indoor conditions, poor click engagement
Full acclimation, verify clicks during install
No expansion gap, pinned under cabinets, transition too tight
Verify gaps, never screw through floor, check all fixed objects
Loose subfloor, debris under floor, inadequate fastening (nail-down)
Fix subfloor first, vacuum before install, proper nail schedule
Back-butter, check coverage, 95%+ for large format
Unlevel substrate, no leveling system, tiles of varying thickness
Flatten substrate, use leveling clips, check tile calibration
Substrate imperfections, thin flooring, inadequate underlayment
Wrong blade, wrong cut direction, dull blade
Fine-tooth blade, decorative side up, score line first
Wet concrete, no vapor barrier, spills not addressed
Test moisture, install barriers, educate customer

procedures.title: Documentation & Signoff
procedures.body: Documentation protects the installer and educates the customer.
steps: Before installation: Photo all substrate conditions, existing damage, moisture readings
During installation: Photo any discovered issues, corrections made, and critical details
After installation: Photo completed floor, transitions, trim details
Create punch list of any items to address
Walk through with customer pointing out features and limitations of their flooring type
Provide care instructions — manufacturer's document plus any specific guidance
Obtain signed acceptance acknowledging walkthrough completed and work accepted

callouts.title: Callback Protection
callouts.content: Photos with timestamps establish what existed before you arrived. Without this documentation, you own every pre-existing condition the customer decides to notice after installation.
